summary: The post is a transcript of a podcast episode centered on Julia Silge, with side remarks from Wes McKinney about tooling, licensing, and LLM-era dynamics. The main throughline is how glue work across team boundaries can drive outsized impact, even when it is undervalued. The discussion then turns to how LLMs have consumed community content and changed the feedback loop that once generated open knowledge, raising sustainability questions for developer support and open-source growth. Posit’s evolving licensing posture is framed as an attempt to balance open-source values with long-term sustainability in a changed ecosystem. The episode concludes with concerns about how new projects will gain adoption if LLMs primarily amplify already-known tools.
key_points: Glue work across organizational boundaries can deliver large user-experience gains despite being thankless or invisible.
LLMs trained on community content reduce new public Q&A, weakening the knowledge-creation loop that supported open tooling.
Licensing is being reconsidered to prevent large platforms from monetizing open-source work without contributing back.
Remote work creates a tension between deep focus and cross-team connection, which leaders must actively manage.
There is a looming adoption challenge for new tools if LLMs prioritize only what they already know.
money_quotes: I've become like a pretty heavy Obsidian user mainly as like a capture tool because like stuff will come up in like a Slack channel that I'm that I'm a part of or over email.
I find like capturing things in Obsidian is like my personal don't forget this list of things to not forget and to get things out of my inbox.
Well, there was like, there was an inflection point where I think the license for content posted on Stack Overflow changed.
The licensing around these kinds of issues is very, it's very, very interesting.
The thing that's been on my mind a lot is if, how will developers be motivated to discover and learn new technologies that their favorite LLM doesn't know about?
How is that going to affect the development of new open-source software projects?
key_insight: LLMs may reshape open-source adoption by privileging already-known tools, raising new sustainability and discovery challenges for emerging projects.
